blocks.body: Interrogative sentences and objections are an important part of English grammar. Here you will learn how to correctly form interrogative sentences (Yes/No questions, Wh-questions), use short answers, create sectional questions (question tags), and also correctly construct negations with different types of verbs.

• Yes/No Questions - general questions: Do you like coffee? Are you ready?
  • Wh-Questions — special questions: What do you want? Where are you going?
  • Question Words — question words: who, what, where, when, why, how.
  • Word order is an auxiliary verb before the subject: Do you...? Is he...? Can they...?
blocks.heading: Types of questions (Question Forms)

blocks.body:
• Short answers - answer with an auxiliary verb: Yes, I do. / No, I don't.
  • With to be - we use the form be: Yes, he is. / No, they aren't.
  • With modals - we repeat the modal verb: Yes, you can. / No, I mustn't.
  • Important - you cannot simply answer "Yes" or "No" in a formal context.
blocks.heading: Short Answers

blocks.body:
• Question Tags — a question at the end of a sentence: You like tea, don't you?
  • Rule — affirmation + negative tag, or denial + positive tag.
  • Examples: She is happy, isn't she? They don't know, do they?
  • Intonation — falling for confirmation, rising for a real question.
blocks.heading: Question Tags

blocks.body:
Topic Level Description
Question Forms A1 How to ask a question
Wh-Questions A1–A2 Special questions (who, what, where...)
Short Answers A1 Short answers to questions
Question Word Order A1–A2 Word order in questions
Question Tags B1 Chapter questions (isn't it?, don't you?)
Subject vs Object Questions B1 Questions about the subject and the application
Indirect Questions B1–B2 Indirect questions (Can you tell me...?)
Negation in Simple A1–A2 Negation with do/does/did + not
Negation with be, modals and have got A1–A2 Negation with different types of verbs
Negative Pronouns A2–B1 Negative pronouns (nobody, nothing, nowhere)
Double Negatives B1 Double negatives and how to avoid them
blocks.heading: Topics in this section

blocks.body:
• With Simple Tenses — do/does/did + not: I don't like it. He doesn't know.
  • With to be - add not: I am not ready. They aren't here.
  • With modals - modal + not: You can't go. She won't come.
  • Negative pronouns — nobody, nothing, nowhere: Nobody knows.
  • Double negatives is a mistake in English: ❌ I don't know nothing. ✓ I don't know anything.
blocks.heading: Negation

blocks.body:
🧠

Start with basic Yes/No questions - they are the easiest to learn.

Remember the word order: auxiliary verb + subject + main verb.

For negatives, use do/does/did + not with main verbs.

Question tags — affirmation with a negative tag, denial with a positive one.

Avoid double negatives - this is a mistake in English!
blocks.heading: Learning tips
